title: Labour market flows, unemployment and the Phillips curve
summary: BIS Working Paper No 1341 introduces a new "unemployment gap" measure that distinguishes between stock-based unemployment (traditional point-in-time count) and flow-based unemployment (rate implied by current labour market transitions). The paper demonstrates empirically using US data that this gap provides better predictive power for future inflation and wage dynamics than traditional slack measures. A theoretical search-and-matching model with nominal wage rigidities and persistent shocks explains why labour market transition flows matter for price and wage behaviour.
key_points: The unemployment gap is defined as the difference between flow-based unemployment (implied by current labour market transitions) and stock-based unemployment (traditional unemployment rate)
A positive unemployment gap (flow-based > stock-based) signals decreasing inflationary pressures, indicating a loosening labour market
A negative unemployment gap (flow-based < stock-based) suggests rising inflationary pressures, indicating a tightening labour market
Transitions into employment (hiring rates and job retention rates) are the primary drivers of the unemployment gap
The theoretical model incorporates nominal wage rigidities while allowing firms to negotiate wages with new hires, making bargaining power endogenous
Persistent changes in labour market flows have strong and long-lasting impacts on wages and prices
JEL classification: E23, E24, E31, E32, E52, J64
why_it_matters: This research provides a more accurate tool for policymakers and analysts to assess labour market conditions and their inflationary implications. Traditional measures like the unemployment rate may mask underlying dynamics in labour market flows that are more predictive of future wage and price behaviour. For central banks calibrating monetary policy, understanding the direction and momentum of labour market changes may provide earlier signals about inflation trajectory than static unemployment measures.
macro_context: This is academic research on labour market dynamics and the Phillips curve. The unemployment gap measure offers an alternative framework for understanding the labour market-inflation relationship. The authors show that flow-based unemployment provides better predictive power for future inflation and wage growth than traditional stock-based measures. The theoretical model demonstrates that firms' wage-setting behaviour depends on both stock and flow measures of unemployment when transition probability shocks exhibit sufficient persistence. This has implications for how monetary policymakers might assess labour market slack.

human_review.notes: Record is accurate, well-supported, and properly captures the source content. All key points are factually correct and consistent with the source. No discrepancies found.

notes: This is a BIS academic working paper (82 pages). The source metadata tags it under 'market structure' but the actual content is macroeconomic research on labour market dynamics and the Phillips curve, not market structure in the sense of trading infrastructure or market operations. Classified as 'macro catalysts' topic as it relates to understanding labour market conditions that influence monetary policy and inflation expectations. The paper is recent (published 2026-03-30, within 90 days of TODAY), providing original empirical and theoretical analysis on US labour market data. Views are those of the authors and do not necessarily reflect BIS or member central bank positions.
